content: Severe symptomatic AS requires valve replacement—no medical therapy improves survival. Options: Surgical AVR (SAVR) or Transcatheter AVR (TAVR). TAVR initially for inoperable/high-risk patients (PARTNER trial), now expanded to all risk categories. PARTNER 3 (low risk) and Evolut Low Risk trials showed TAVR noninferior or superior to SAVR for death/stroke/rehospitalization at 1-2 years. Current guidelines: TAVR preferred for age >80 or life expectancy <10 years or high surgical risk; SAVR preferred for age <65 or life expectancy >20 years or need for concomitant cardiac surgery; either reasonable for ages 65-80 (shared decision-making). TAVR advantages: less invasive, shorter recovery, lower bleeding/AKI risk; disadvantages: higher pacemaker rate, paravalvular leak, uncertain long-term durability (>10 years). SAVR advantages: proven durability (>20 years), lower pacemaker/leak rates; disadvantages: sternotomy, longer recovery, more bleeding/AF. Mechanical valves require warfarin; bioprosthetic avoid anticoagulation but degenerate over time. Pre-procedure evaluation: coronary angiography, peripheral vascular access assessment, CT annulus sizing, heart team discussion.
